beats.narration: When a student's code breaks, the fastest help usually just hands them a fixed line. The bug gets solved; the understanding doesn't. This is Socratic Code Tutor. It never gives you the answer. It asks the question that gets you there yourself.

This runs for real: Python, compiled and executed with Pyodide, right in the browser. No student code ever touches our server. It fails with an IndexError on line four. A normal assistant would just tell me to fix that line. Watch what this one does instead.

Every reply is generated live by GPT-5.6, but it's structurally blocked from ever handing over a fix. A visible four-rung ladder lets the student escalate one rung at a time: a conceptual question, then localization, then naming the mechanism, and at rung four, a near-solution description that's still not runnable code. Every response is screened server-side, after generation and before it reaches this chat.
This trace isn't simulated. It's captured live from Python's own sys-dot-settrace inside the Pyodide sandbox, so the student can step through exactly what their variables actually did.

Every session gets tagged with a misconception category by GPT-5.6 — off-by-one, mutation versus copy, scope confusion — so a teacher sees the pattern across a whole class, stored right in the browser, and can export it as JSON or Markdown.

The spec was written up front. Codex, driving GPT-5.6, then implemented the whole application in one continuous session: the Pyodide runner, the trace capture, the guardrail pipeline, and the teacher export. A four-part audit found real guardrail bypasses; Codex fixed every one in a single follow-up pass. GPT-5.6 does two jobs here — it's the model Codex used to write the code, and it's the model running the tutor and the tagging live, right now.
Socratic Code Tutor. Education track, OpenAI Build Week. Link's below.
